coredata.dc:title: An LTCC monolithic microreactor for the synthesis of carbon dots with photoluminescence imaging of the reaction progress
coredata.dc:creator: Berenguel-Alonso, M.
Ortiz-Gómez, I.
Fernández, B.
Couceiro, P.
Alonso-Chamarro, J.
Capitán-Vallvey, L.F.
Salinas-Castillo, A.
Puyol, M.
coredata.dc:description: This work describes the development of a fully integrated Low Temperature Co-fired Ceramic (LTCC) microreactor for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDots). The microreactor integrates fluidics, a heating resistor and an optical window for fluorescence imaging of the reaction progress in a monolithic, all ceramic device, ensuring homogeneous surface chemistry and physical properties. A pool of different CDots was synthesized at high temperature and pressure using a hydrothermal method, demonstrating the robustness of the microreactor. The synthesis was monitored by following the photoluminescence of the produced CDots, and the reaction conditions were optimized according to their Quantum Yield (QY) and the flow pattern inside the microchannel. The obtained CDots exhibited blue photoluminescence upon irradiation with UV light with QYs of up to 0.77. The CDots were screened as metal nanoprobes and bioimaging contrast agents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Microreactor
Synthesis process intensification
CDots
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture and live cell imaging

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministerio de Economía, Industria y Competitividad, Gobierno de España

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the fabrication process of the LTCC microreactor. This process enables the fabrication of a monolithic device integrating the fluidics (including a 3D hydrodynamic flow focus inlet structure) and the heating resistor. Furthermore, LTCC thin layers are used as optical windows for fluorescence monitoring of the reaction product, thus avoiding the problems related to the integration of other materials.

A) 3D representation of the microreactor channel and heating resistor for Carbon Dot synthesis. The fluidic elements (represented in different shades of blue and purple) consist of three inlets, a spiral-shaped downstream microreactor channel, and an outlet. B) Top view of the microreactor, including the inlet/outlet tubes. C) Bottom view of the microreactor, with the integrated temperature sensor and the contact pads for the heating resistor. D) Cross-section microscope photograph of the LTCC microreactor, showing the downstream microchannel (five spiral turns) and the screen-printed gold resistor. E) Photograph of the open microchannels and F) close-up of the middle inlet and the two sheath flow channels.
Optimization of the reaction conditions, i.e. pressure and temperature. Comparison of the QY of the obtained Carbon Dots under each set of conditions.

Photographs taken during the synthesis of Carbon Dots at different conditions under UV light excitation. A) Varying pressure at a constant temperature of 190 °C. B) Varying temperature at a constant pressure of 17 bar.
Absorption (continuous lines) and Photoluminescence (dashed lines) spectra of the obtained Carbon Dots.

A) Photoluminescence spectra of Carbon Dots-PEI in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+ ions (0, 0.5, 2.0, 5.0, 10, 25 and 100 μM) and Stern-Volmer plot (inset). B) Selectivity of the CDots-PEI for Cu2+ over other ionic species at a concentration of 100 μM.

A) Photoluminescence spectra of Carbon Dots-ED in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ ions (0, 0.5, 5,0, 50 and 500 μM) and Stern-Volmer plot (inset). B) Selectivity of the CDots-ED for Hg2+ over other ionic species at a concentration of 400 μM.

Biological study of the Carbon Dots in HEK293 cells. The images were taken with a confocal microscope and therefore ensure that the Carbon Dots are inside the cells. A) Transmitted light brightfield image of HEK293 cells. B) Superposed transmitted light brightfield and photoluminescence images of the HEK293 cells loaded with CDots-ED. C) Cytotoxicity assay results.

head.ce:title: An LTCC monolithic microreactor for the synthesis of carbon dots with photoluminescence imaging of the reaction progress

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Sensors & Biosensors Group, Dept. of Chemistry, Universitat Autònoma de Barcelona, Bellaterra, E08193 Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: ECsens, Dept. of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Sciences, University of Granada, E18071 Granada, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dept. of Physiology, School of Pharmacy, University of Granada, Granada E18071, Spain

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work describes the development of a fully integrated Low Temperature Co-fired Ceramic (LTCC) microreactor for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDots). The microreactor integrates fluidics, a heating resistor and an optical window for fluorescence imaging of the reaction progress in a monolithic, all ceramic device, ensuring homogeneous surface chemistry and physical properties. A pool of different CDots was synthesized at high temperature and pressure using a hydrothermal method, demonstrating the robustness of the microreactor. The synthesis was monitored by following the photoluminescence of the produced CDots, and the reaction conditions were optimized according to their Quantum Yield (QY) and the flow pattern inside the microchannel. The obtained CDots exhibited blue photoluminescence upon irradiation with UV light with QYs of up to 0.77. The CDots were screened as metal nanoprobes and bioimaging contrast agents.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent nanoparticles have attracted significant attention in recent decades due to their potential applications in (bio)sensing, bioimaging and energy cells. For instance, semiconductor nanocrystals, well known as Quantum Dots (QDs) have been thoroughly researched because of their outstanding optical properties and outstanding potential in medical and bioimaging applications. [1] However, these types of nanoparticles have some drawbacks, such as toxicity (due to their heavy metal content), that hinder their further application. In recent years, CDots have emerged as a suitable alternative to such QDs because of their comparable optical properties and the added benefits of biocompatibility, low toxicity, chemical stability, well-known carbon chemistry and low cost [2–5].

CDots can be synthesized using top-down (breaking down carbon structures) and bottom-up approaches (building up from molecules). However, batch syntheses of nanoparticles generally present poor reproducibility and, therefore, the properties of the product vary significantly from batch to batch. Microfluidic reactor technology can improve the synthetic processes not only by better controlling shape and size distribution, [6] but also by minimizing energy consumption, reagent volumes and waste production [7]. The smaller reactor size also contributes to more efficient and controlled heat and mass transfer, and thus, to a more homogeneous particle distribution within a batch.

LTCC microreactors have demonstrated great potential in the syntheses of nanomaterials due to their chemical inertness, the wide working temperature range and the possibility of fabricating complex multilayer structures. [8–10] LTCCs are compatible with screen-printing techniques [11], which enables the integration of electronic elements such as heaters [10,12] or electro-chemical detection systems [13]. Furthermore, LTCC technology does not require clean room facilities, thereby decreasing fabrication costs.

However, opacity is one of the main disadvantages regarding the further application of LTCC devices, not only in microreactor synthesis, but also in Micro Total Analysis Systems (μTAS). [14] Particularly in microreactor synthesis, the opacity of the material prevents the visualization of the reaction progress, which is of much importance to understand the behavior of the fluid streams, especially in the case of complex flow patterns (such as droplets) or harsh reaction conditions (for instance, reactions above the boiling point of the solvent). To overcome this limitation, hybrid devices incorporating transparent materials have been developed using, for instance, glass [15,16] or PDMS [17,18]. These approaches require complex, time-consuming post-fabrication processes to seal the microfluidic device or optical path. Furthermore, the different properties of the sealing material (thermal expansion coefficient, chemical stability) may limit the use of these devices, especially in synthetic applications in which harsh environments, organic solvents and/or high temperatures are often required. Previous work by our research group presented the integration of thin ceramic layers as optical windows for absorbance detection [19] as well as the fabrication of monolithic microfluidic platforms suitable for fluorescence imaging [20,21].

Few previous studies have dealt with the synthesis of CDots in microreactors. Lu and colleagues [22] synthesized CDots with various carbon precursors and solvents using a 1 mm Teflon tube submerged in an oil bath. Rao et al. [23] recently performed a synthesis of CDots in a microreactor with a single inlet (mixing the precursors outside). More recently, Tang et al. [24] presented a modular microreactor including a layer of foamy copper for the synthesis of CDots with QY of up to 84% and Rao et al. developed a microreactor with porous copper fibers for synthesizing nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) [25]. Our research group reported a modular LTCC microreactor consisting of two separate units: a micro-fluidic platform and a heating module [26]. In the case of the latter, the CDots in DMSO as solvent exhibited blue photoluminescence and QYs of up to 0.03. The synthesis was highly reproducible owing to the temperature control, and the CDots were investigated as pH sensors and bioimaging contrast agents.

This article presents hydrothermal syntheses of highly photoluminescent CDots in an LTCC monolithic microreactor, which integrates an optical window and a heater. The optical window makes it possible to monitor the reaction in the entire microchannel by following the photoluminescence of the reaction product. Moreover, the optical window can withstand the harsh reaction conditions (up to 17 bar and 190 °C). This monolithic microreactor avoids the need to integrate other materials and ensures homogeneous surface chemistry and physical properties. Different CDots are synthesized and their optical properties characterized, with QYs of up to 0.77. The CDots are screened as nanoprobes for heavy metal detection and bioimaging contrast agents.

All reagents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Barcelona, Spain) and used as received, without any further purification, unless otherwise stated. Polyethyleneimine, branched, MW 10,000 99% (Alfa Aesar) was purchased from Fisher Scientific (Madrid, Spain).

For all the different syntheses of CDots, 0.5764 g of citric acid was dissolved in 15 mL of water and loaded in a gastight glass syringe (Hamilton series Gastight 1000 TL L). Different nitrogen source precursors were prepared for each synthesis: 200 μL of ethylenediamine was dissolved in 15 mL of water, 325 μL of diethylenetriamine was dissolved in 15 mL of water, and finally, 0.1441 g of polyethyleneimine was dissolved in 15 mL of water. Two gastight glass syringes were filled with the same nitrogen source precursor for a given synthesis. All solutions were prepared in double distilled water and de-gassed before being introduced into the syringes. The syringes were mounted on three syringe pumps (TSE Systems 540060) and connected to the microreactor using Teflon tubes (0.8 mm i.d., Tecnyfluor, Spain). The connection between the tubes and the microreactor was secured using O-rings (FPM75, Epidor, Spain) and pressure was applied by means of a custom-built aluminum connector.

The microreactor presented here monolithically integrates the fluidics and a screen-printed heating element. LTCC 951 green tapes in different thicknesses were used to fabricate the microreactor: DuPont 951PX and DuPont 951 P T (254 and 114 μm thick prior to firing, respectively). The heating element consisted of a screen-printed gold resistor made out of DuPont 5742 gold co-fireable conductor paste. DuPont 6141 silver co-fireable paste was used to fill the vias and create the contact pads. The microfluidic channels were cut in the green tapes using an IR laser (LPKF Protolaser 200, Garbsen, Germany). Once the different LTCC layers were laser cut and/or screen-printed, they were aligned and thermolaminated using a hydraulic press (Talleres Francisco Camps, Granollers, Spain). The device was sintered in a programmable box furnace (Carbolite CBCWF11/23P16, Afora, Spain) in air atmosphere following the two-step thermal profile recommended by DuPont, consisting of a 1 h organic burnout at 350 °C and 1 h firing at 850 °C. A class A PT100 temperature sensor (Innovative Sensor Technology, Switzerland) was then glued to the microreactor using epoxy paste (EPO-TEK H20E, Billerica, MA, USA). The temperature was controlled using a PID system implemented on a PIC18F4431 microcontroller, [12] receiving the sensor input.

UV/Vis absorption spectra were obtained using a Shimadzu UV-310PC UV–vis-NIR double beam scanning spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan). Photoluminescence spectra were obtained with a Fluorolog 1 Modular spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France). QYs were calculated with the optically dilute measurement method, [27] using quinine sulphate in 0.1 M sulphuric acid as a standard and applying the following equation.
ϕ x
=
ϕ
s t
∙
I x
I
s t
∙
A
s t
A x
∙
η x 2
η
s t
2
Where φ is the photoluminescence QY, I the integrated area of the corrected emission spectrum, A the absorbance and η the refractive index of the solvent. The subindexes x and st refer to the sample and the reference standard, respectively. Given the fact that both standard and sample were dissolved in water, the last term of the equation was ignored. QYs were calculated as synthesized without purification.

CDots were evaluated as nanoprobes for metal ion detection. For these studies, CDs were dialyzed against Milli-Q water using spectra/pro-dialysis membrane with a cutoff of 1 kDa for their purification. A fluorescence cuvette was filled with 2.5 mL of blank/sample and 0.5 mL of CDots. The concentration of CDots was previously adjusted to obtain an absorption of ca. 0.05 a.u. after mixing with the sample, and thus avoiding undesired autoabsorption. The blank consisted of phosphate buffer at pH 7.4. Metal ion samples were prepared by weighting the metal salt and dissolving it in the same buffer. Different metal ion concentrations were obtained by diluting the stock solutions with phosphate buffer. The cuvette was gently shaken by hand to ensure complete mixing. The photoluminescence spectrum was then registered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and live cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: To culture embryonic kidney 293 cells (HEK297), the cells were grown in DMEM high glucose including 10% foetal calf serum at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Incubation with CDots was performed under culturing conditions. CDots were then diluted 104-fold in the culturing medium. For live cell imaging, the cells were mounted in coverslips in Leibovitz L-15 medium including 10% foetal calf serum. The cells were imaged on a LSM 510 Meta (ZEISS, Jena, Germany) equipped with ZEN 2009 software, using a heatable Insert P (PeCon, Erbach, Germany) to maintain the temperature at 37 °C. CDots were excited with a 405 nm laser and the resulting fluorescence was collected from 450 to 750 nm for spectroscopy and from 450 to 550 nm for imaging. The scanner and detector were mounted on an inverted microscope (X) equipped with an oil immersion X-numerical aperture 60x objective (Zeiss). To stain the membranes, the cells were pre-stained in protein free media with 0.005% (w·v-1) Evans blue. Evans blue was excited with a 514 nm laser line and its fluorescence was recorded using a 585 nm long pass filter.

The microreactor for the synthesis of CDots was fabricated using LTCC technology. It consisted of a monolithic ceramic block, integrating fluidics, a heating element and the necessary pads for electrical connection. The microreactor had a circular shape with a diameter of 6 cm and a total thickness of 3 mm. The microreactor design was based on a multilayer approach. The fabrication process is depicted in Fig. 1 .

The microreactor can be separated into two units, namely the fluidics and the heating resistor (see Fig. 2 A). The fluidic unit contained all the microchannels and the optical window for fluorescence monitoring of the reaction. Fluidics consisted of three inlets for the injection of the different reagents, a spiral-shaped downstream microreactor channel and an outlet channel. The multilayer fabrication approach made it possible to obtain structures like the 3D hydrodynamic flow focusing inlet system (see Fig. 2E and F), which confines the middle reagent stream at the center of the fluidic channel, thus reducing the possibility of precipitation of the solid reaction products on the walls and avoiding channel clogging. [28] The total volume of the microreactor channel was about 75 μL, with a total length of 630 mm and cross-section dimensions of 525 μm wide times 216 μm high (see the cross-section image of the microreactor in Fig. 2D). The optical window consisted of a single LTCC layer, and therefore no other materials needed to be integrated in the microreactor. In this way, the fabrication process was kept simpler. Different LTCC layers were evaluated as optical windows according to their thicknesses (43 μm DuPont 951C2, 97 μm DuPont 951 P T and 216 μm DuPont 951PX, thicknesses given are after firing). As expected, the light transmittance decreased with the increasing window thickness, and it was found that the thicker window (216 μm) did not have any noticeable transmittance. Although the 43 μm thick window exhibited the highest transmittance, it was discarded due to its higher fragility considering the harsh experimental conditions employed during the synthesis (up to 190 °C and 17 bar).

The heating element was screen-printed on the side opposite to the fluidic inlets/outlet, and occupying the same area as the microreaction channel in order to reach the working temperatures and have a temperature gradient as low possible, as seen elsewhere. [12] The final microreactor is shown in Fig. 2B and C.

The synthesis of CDots presented here is based on that proposed by Zhu and colleagues, [29] in which citric acid and ethylenediamine were first condensed and then carbonized by a hydrothermal method to produce N-doped CDots with a high QY. In our case, citric acid was pumped into the microreactor through Inlet 2, while ethylenediamine was pumped through Inlets 1 and 3, thus confining the citric acid to a cylindrical fluid stream in the center of the ethylenediamine (see Fig. 2A). Pressure of the microreactor was adjusted by a Back-Pressure Regulator connected to the outlet, where different cartridges could be used. Measured pressure corresponds to the pressure of the Back-Pressure Regulator, when liquid can flow through the microsystem and is able to leak out. The maximum pressure achieved was limited by the operational range of the syringe pumps used. The reagents were pumped at a total flow rate of 10 μL/min (3.33 μL/min each inlet) to achieve a relatively long reaction time inside the microreactor, while obtaining the product in a reasonable time. [26]

Regarding temperature, Fig. 3B shows a dramatic effect of temperature on the QY of the CDots obtained with a maximum at 190 °C. Additionally, this is clearly observed in the photographs in Fig. 4B, in which the photoluminescence of the CDots formed in the reaction is greatly increased by higher temperatures.

To demonstrate the versatility of the microreactor, different N-sources were used to produce a pool of different CDots under the optimized pressure and temperature conditions. The N-sources used were ethylenediamine, diethylenetriamine and polyethyleneimine. The obtained CDots, namely CDots-ED, CDots-DETA and CDots-PEI, exhibited QYs of 0.77, 0.76 and 0.17, respectively. These values agree with others reported in the literature. [29–31]

The CDots exhibited a bright blue photoluminescence upon irradiation with a UV lamp (see photographs in Fig. 4). All the different CDots synthesized showed similar absorbance and photoluminescence spectra, with maxima around 350 and 450 nm, respectively, as seen in Fig. 5 . QYs were calculated for the CDots synthesized with the different nitrogen sources, giving values of 0.77 for CDots-ED, 0.76 for CDots-DETA and 0.17 for CDots-PEI respectively. These values, the absorbance and photoluminescence spectra are comparable to those of other reported CDots synthesized in batch under similar conditions [29–31] or in a microreactor based on copper fibers [25].

The size of the CDots ranged from 2.2 to 4.3 nm, as seen in the TEM images in Fig. 6 ). However, it was very challenging to obtain TEM images of the CDots (as in similar syntheses [30,32]) and the populations found were not big enough to extract representative values. Recent studies on the mechanism of this reaction and the photoluminescence mechanism of the CDots suggest the formation of polymer dots, when the carbonization is not complete, as well as carbon core nanoparticles [30,33–35]. The polymer dots had molecular-like fluorophore moieties embedded, contributing to the high QY observed. In this case, the composition of the product appeared shifted towards the polymer dots, thus explaining the high QY obtained and the difficulty in obtaining TEM images, given their amorphous nature. Increasing the temperature of the reaction would lead to higher carbonization and, consequently, to a carbon core-based product, usually more photostable, but with a lower QY. [30]

Synthesized CDots were screened as metal sensors by studying the photoluminescence quenching in the presence of metal ions. The different CDots behave differently in the presence of metal ions. CDots-PEI showed strong photoluminescence quenching in the presence of Cu2+ (see Fig. 7 A), while no variation was observed in the case of CDots-ED and CDots-DETA. Similarly, Hg2+ ions produced photoluminescence quenching only on CDots-ED (see Fig. 8 A).

Different concentrations of the quenching species were used in both cases to evaluate the analytical performance of this method. The relative photoluminescence intensity was plotted against the quencher concentration, according to the Stern-Volmer equation:
I 0
I
= 1 +
K
S V
Q
Where I0 and I are the intensities of the CDots without and with the quenching species Q, and KSV is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. CDots-PEI showed a good linear correlation in the studied range, as seen in the inset Stern-Volmer plot, and the detection limit (calculated as LOD = 3SD/KSV ) was found to be 1.22 μM Cu2+.

The selectivity of both CDots-PEI and CDots-ED for Cu2+ and Hg2+, respectively, was assessed by studying the photoluminescence response of the CDots in the presence of different heavy metals and cationic species usually present in water samples. Their photoluminescence was only slightly affected by the presence of the potential interfering ions (see Figs. 7B and 8 B), demonstrating the suitable application of the synthesized CDots for the analysis of Cu2+ and Hg2+ using CDots-PEI and CDots-ED, respectively, as the selective optical detection tools.

We investigated the suitability of CDots-ED as cell markers, since they exhibited the highest QY of the different CDots synthesized. For this purpose, CDs were purified as explained in Section 2.4. The confocal microscopy study revealed that HEK293 cells were able to uptake CDots-ED, as seen in Fig. 9 . The cell viability was studied by means of an MTT test, a cell metabolic activity experiment. The results showed a slight decrease in cell viability compared to the control sample. However, viability remained above 80% in all the studied concentrations after 24 h. Even after 48 h, viability remained above that threshold at relatively high CDots-ED concentrations of up to 1 mg/mL. These results demonstrate a comparable or better biocompatibility to similar CDots [30] as well as graphene quantum dots (Yuan et al. 2014) [36].

A fully integrated microreactor has been developed, incorporating fluidics, a heating resistor and an optical window for the fluorescence imaging of the reaction progress. The described procedure enables the fabrication of a monolithic microfluidic reactor with homogeneous surface chemistry and physical properties and is suitable for mass-scale production. The optical window was key in being able to study the optimal reaction conditions and ensure a continuous, stable flow pattern by following the photoluminescence of the reaction product.

The microreactor was used to synthesize a pool of different CDots by means of a hydrothermal method, using citric acid and different N-sources. The optimal reaction conditions were found to be 190 °C and 17 bar, enabling the production of highly photoluminescent CDots under harsh conditions. Preliminary studies showed the potential applicability of the CDots as nanoprobes for heavy metal detection and as bioimaging contrast agents, due to their low cytotoxicity.

The results described in this work help to establish LTCC as a go-to material in microreactor applications, especially where high temperature/pressure, organic solvents or strong chemicals are required. The possibility of visualizing the flow inside the microchannels under certain circumstances, eliminates one drawback for the further application of the material. This reinforces the inherent properties of LTCC such as chemical inertness, a high-temperature working range, mechanical strength, 3D-structuring capabilities and the simple integration of conductive layers or scalable production, making this very attractive for the development of microfluidic devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Government of Catalonia [FI programme and 2017SGR220); the Regional Government of Andalusia [P10-FQM-5974]; MINECO [CTQ2012-36165, CTQ2016-78754-C2-1-R, CTQ2017-85011-R]; and Reincorporación de Doctores programme sponsored by the University of Granada.
